speaker: interviewer
text: Take number three. Interview of Claire Boren, August 14th, 1996. Claire, how did you meet your husband? It was a blind date. And we met, and it went very fast. I mean, we met the end of May, and we were talking about getting married, I think, like within a month or two. And I got my engagement ring that August. And we were married that December of '61. And would you please state his full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Adam Boren.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Did someone-- who was the person who set up the blind date?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. There are a few people who sort of lay claim to that. Somebody said from the family. He says that a friend of his met me at a party and gave him my name. I don't know, but it was a blind date.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what point in your relationship did you share your Holocaust experiences with Adam who is also a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, but I think over time we would just talk about different incidents. And I always-- Adam used to talk about his experiences publicly. He started at an early point. I never did. I always thought that I did, but I didn't. Sometimes friends of mine would say, I never knew this. And I always thought that I did. I really didn't come to think in terms of my past until, I guess, I was ready. Rabbi Yitz Greenberg had organized the first meeting of the second generation. I think it was at NYU. It was either in 1974 or '75. It was a two-day conference. There were people from all over the world and Israel. And for the first time, I heard talk of what it means to be a survivor but primarily a second generation. And it was a real awakening for me. And at that point, I decided that-- that I wanted somehow to go into the field of working with either survivors or second generation. And as I said, that must have been mid '70s. And then '81 is when I decided to go back to graduate school and become a social worker.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Before we talk about graduate school, let's just fill out one more aspect of your family. You and Adam have two children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us their names and when they were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sari was born in 1964, August 11. And she is named after Adam's mother and sister, Sarah and Mina. Jonathan is February 11, 1970. And he's named after my father, Yosef Jonathan. And Adam's father, Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, now, let's go back to your career goals. After you attended this conference at NYU, you decided to change gears and go back to graduate school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your career goals at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just wanted to find some way to learn how to work in this field. And there was no specific-- I inquired-- there was nobody that was giving a course or doing anything on how to work with survivors or second generation. After-- so I became-- I decided the best route for me would to just go to social work school and then acquire other training.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go to social work--

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Columbia University.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I attended one of those conferences-- I think it was the Psychiatric Institute, whatever it was-- another one that also gave like a-- a conference on second generation. I remember there was a therapist who stood up there and said that she had been in therapy for seven years, and the therapist had never touched upon the fact that she was a survivor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I think it was just about this point in the mid '70s that people were first beginning to talk about survivors, second generations, the-- what was going on. And after graduate school, about a year or two later, I heard that Eve-- Eva Fogelman, she's been active. She's a second generation. She's done a film and things.

speaker: interviewee
text: She and-- through this Psychoanalytic Institute were setting up a program. It was a two-year program-- how to work with groups of second generation. And I attended that group. The other person who was in that was Vladka Meed's daughter-in-law was in that group. Of course, I can't remember her name right now. But I did that. And part of that learning how to work with second generation, we ourselves had to be in a psychoanalytic group. And it was during this period that I really started to think in terms of my past and thinking about it. It was during this period-- that's right-- that I was interviewed by Judith Kastenberg. And it was the therapist who ran this group who urged me to start talking-- to talk in front of schools or other places, to start talking about my past. So we're now talking about-- this is now mid '80s. And when I started talking about it, that's when some of my friends would say, I never knew that. And I would say, well, I thought I told you. And they said, no. Apparently, I had never really talked about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: As you were growing up in the United States, did your mother and stepfather ever talk to you about the war years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever ask them?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother could never talk about anything because as soon as she would start talking about my family or my father, she would start crying. So I couldn't talk about it. Although back in-- I'm just trying to think-- when Sari was 12, so that was like 20 years ago. So we're talking about '76 or '77, whatever that was. We went-- we were going on a family trip to Israel. And just before I left, my mother told me that there was a cousin of mine in Israel. I didn't know of her existence. I didn't know she had survived. She was my father's oldest brother's daughter. I remember being, like, shocked that I thought there was no one. But here was this woman who was my only living first cousin. And my parents didn't even, like, make a big deal about it. I was, like, upset. I said, of course I want to meet this woman and find out about her. And she's the one who's provided me with a lot of the information, you know, about the family. But it was a very, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very traumatic experience for me to think that my parents didn't think I'd want to meet this woman. And when I met her, it was just, I remember, I was just-- it was a devastating experience because that really-- well, here were-- it was like my past. All of a sudden, this was, you know, my father that nobody would talk to me about, that for years I had to keep a secret, that I couldn't-- I couldn't stand up a synagogue and say yahrseit for him openly. It was a horrible thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did your father-- your stepfather and mother, you know, keep this a secret in the United States in the early years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, they made up the papers, for whatever reason, that I was their child, that they were married before the war. They also said that they were born somewhere else because there were always these rumors that if you-- the quota to come to the United States, you should say this instead of that. Whatever it was, that's how it went. And they were just paranoid about it, you know. They just-- I was not allowed to tell anybody that this was my stepfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what made it so horrible was that I couldn't talk about my biological father. I couldn't keep his memory alive. And Adam was really the one-- and I think that's what makes it easier being married to a survivor-- he said to me, what are you doing? You're killing your father a second time by not allowing his memory to be out there, by not openly talking about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all this came to the forefront when we went to Israel and I met Miriam.

speaker: interviewer
text: At what point did you share your Holocaust experiences with your children? Do you recall a specific incident that in which you told them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think I ever talked to them that much about it. I think Adam did a lot. I think, you know, to some extent my kids were overexposed. You know, I've been studying things about how second generation children fare. And they say the family where they don't talk at all isn't any good. The family where they talk too much isn't good. It's somewhere in the middle. My kids were-- because Adam was so exposed about it, we were always in the paper about it. So, you know, when they played a movie or something, they would come and interview us. And we were like the official Holocaust survivor family. And I think it was, to some extent, it was a bit much. I don't know. I think one incidence, if I had to do it over again, I would do differently. That was our daughter's bat mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you do differently?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she was an excellent student, and this should have been her day and her joyous day. And the rabbi asked us if he could talk about the Holocaust. And he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he talked about all the relatives who should have been there and weren't there. So it was a moving speech, but in years-- years later, I said to myself, everybody's in there crying. Who's the star? Not Sari whose bat mitzvah it is. It's the Holocaust. I would never do that again. Do you ever dream about your Holocaust experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I know Adam does. He, for years, had horrible dreams. I mean, he would wake up-- he would wake up. I would wake up. He'd be hitting me in the dreams. It was terrible. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think has been the strongest influence the Holocaust has had on your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the profession for sure. I mean, I specifically went into it to try to work with survivors, second generation. I don't know. It's hard to say. It's so much part of the fabric of who I am. I'm just, I think, who I am because of my experience. I could never [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] be any different. I don't-- who's to say what I would have been like. But I certainly, I think, I'm-- I think I'm generally an optimistic person who still believes in the goodness of people. But there is a part of me that, you know, was just very different.

speaker: interviewer
text: How have you used your artistic talents in recent months to express your Holocaust experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think that recently, first, when Adam became ill and, you know, my world sort of seemed to fall apart just like a second time, and then, you know, in thinking in terms of today, it-- it just came out. The-- my pieces were dark. Years ago I used to do watercolors, and I always liked doing things outside, you know, sitting in front of Victorian houses and painting them, sitting in front of flower gardens and painting flower gardens. And all my watercolors are happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I'm doing this very somber, heavy work. OK. What do you feel the world should learn from your Holocaust experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, I guess I just want everybody to know that it happened. I think part of it is to understand that the kind of life that I had, it was like what's sometimes depicted. I mean, we had a very nice house. You know, we lived a comfortable life. We had servants. And then all of this, I mean, not that this matters, but all of this was just totally disrupted. Two years ago, Adam and I and our daughter went back to Mizocz. I had no memory of the town. I think if I had gone inside the house, I would have remembered it. But here was a town that was predominantly Jewish, you know, like 90%. There isn't a single Jewish person there. The town looks the same as it did, like, 60 years ago. They still don't have running water. The streets are still cobblestones. I remember my daughter's reaction-- Sari's reaction. She looked at this road. A trip that should have taken us like an hour took us like five hours or something. She said, I can't believe that the Germans came to this little village. I mean, it's a very pretty, peaceful little village. She says, I can't believe they would bother to come into this cobblestone village to eliminate the very last Jew. And I think this is what everybody has to understand-- that they went everywhere and that they were not alone-- that the people who did all the killing in the woods when I was in hiding-- we were with a group of about 30 Jewish people. My mother, I, and these two men who live in Israel survived. They were killed by the Ukrainians, by the local population, by people that my mother recognized. That's who came into the woods. And I think that's what people have to understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Claire, I want to thank you for taking part in the interview. It's been an honor to interview you today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. In this picture in the upper right-hand corner is a picture of my father. I saw one other picture, but this is really the only one that I have. And on the bottom row, the second from the right, that's my grandmother. And the one next to her is one of my aunts, but I'm not sure which one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the rest of the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rest of the family, I don't know who they are. I know those are probably my cousins and some of my uncles, but I don't know who's who.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, would you say the full name of your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yosef Langer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't even remember her first name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have it written down somewhere. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this your maternal grandfather, uh, grandmother.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this is my father's mother. This is my father's family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you-- and an aunt, a first or second name? Do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was Malka was one of the aunts and Tamma. Tamma was the one who lived with us in the same house.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years ago we went back to Mizocz. And from that description of where my house-- where I thought my house was from what my mother had told me, I think the front house was my grandmother's house. And the house in the back was our house. And when I walked to the back of my house, there was a stoop there in the back. And that's the part that I recognized because I remembered playing on the stoop. So I'm pretty sure that this was the house. This is a picture of an Eschwege, where I belonged to the Betar. That's a picture above there of Jabotinsky. And in my handwriting on the right-hand corner, I identify it as the youth group of Betar. And on the other side, it says, in the camp of Eschwege. If you go directly above the drum, and-- that's a picture of me, sort of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which row would you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's, I guess, the second row up from the top or second row down. I'm sort of centered there with my hat and the tie and the whole Betar uniform. This is another picture in Eschwege in the DP camp. I'm the second one in from the right, wearing my Betar uniform. I'm here with some friends. This was probably taken in '49. In the background are one of the houses in the DP camp. I'm the second one in from the right. This is a picture of my sister Bina who was born in the DP camp in Eschwege in 1947, January 23.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how old do you think she is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few months. Probably a half a year old. I had a lot of fun taking care of her. This is a picture of my family probably taken in Eschwege in-- in 1948. And that's myself, my mother, my stepfather, and my sister as a baby, Bina.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken the first year that I was in the United States. It's in the sixth grade. This was in Sunnyside, Queens. And I'm the student standing up. For whatever reason, they chose me to be the one to stand up when they took this photograph. There I am with my braids. This is a wedding picture of Adam and myself when we got married on December 10, 1961 in Queens in the synagogue in Kissena. And that's sort of the farewell shot for our wedding album. This is a family shot that was taken a few years ago, I think, when we were attending a wedding. Starting at the left is our son Jonathan, daughter Sari, myself, and Adam. Those earrings and the cigarette case are the ones that my mother had gone back to the town to dig up. And they're very precious to me because it's the only thing I have left of my family. The earrings belonged to my father's mother, to my grandmother. And it's, you know, something just so tangible of something of my past. I've never worn them. I'm just so afraid of losing them. But maybe one day I will. And I-- they will go to my daughter. And the cigarette case is the case that my mother had made up for my father when they became engaged. It was her engagement gift to him. And the scroll on the left-hand corner-- those are his initials in gold.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which are--

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess it's Y-L. It looks like there might be a third initial even, but I don't know exactly what it is. But those were his initials intertwined.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's going to go to Jonathan. But it's just been-- lately, I've kept it out. I used to always just keep it in the bank vault. I was so afraid of somebody stealing it or losing it. But now I keep it out. It's nice for me to sort of be in touch with it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to do watercolors. And they were always-- I would do them on site, either landscapes, you know, pretty gardens or Victorian houses. Very happy colors. But recently, I've been doing collage. And more recently, this past week, I was in the workshop, and I found myself doing these things without realizing even what I was doing. And obviously, this resonates for me about my past. If you look at all the dark colors and the nails, the bloody nails, and the rope and all of the other things which I wasn't even aware of what I was doing. But it certainly is a, I think, a disturbing picture that to me symbolizes of some of the things that I went through. I started on this piece with that small, rusted, copper piece that I had found and the circle that was sort of a circle. Now, I could have placed them in any which way. But I chose to put it on this piece of black wood and then the circle underneath it. And as I was doing that, I realized that what I was really working out here was that hanging that had witnessed as a child. And then on the right side, I started scratching out a figure, the hanging figure. But then I said, oh, that's getting to be a little too much, too morbid, too whatever. So I scrubbed it out a little, but I left it in. And this definitely reworks for me the memory of looking out that window and seeing that person hanging on the gallows in the middle of the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Claire, would you introduce your family for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, Adam, and my son, Jonathan.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And Sammy, our dog, our shared dog, Sammy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Our shared dog, Sammy. All right. Adam, when did you first know of your wife's Holocaust experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that I've learned-- I've met Claire in 1961. And we were married six months later. About half way through, three months after I knew her, she told me about her experiences.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you feel that knowledge has affected-- influenced your relationship?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I think that basically I was very lucky to have married Claire. One is that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she understands without talking of my experiences and so do I of hers. An outsider, if I married a girl, a person that was not a Holocaust survivor, would be very difficult to understand some of my behavior or some of my reaction and vice versa because of the experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Jonathan, how do you feel-- rather let me rephrase that. At what point did you first learn of your parents' experiences in the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I was very young. I don't really remember. I mean, it comes up pretty early. I couldn't remember exactly what age. And ever since I can remember, I guess, it's always been an issue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. And how do you feel this has affected your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: In, like, every-- like I had said before, the kind of time that would take to get into is basically my whole life. So it affects everything you do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what lessons do you feel, Jonathan, that the world should learn from the experiences of your parents in the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- that I-- it's different to every-- everybody for what they believe. You know, so-- it's like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. At this point, Claire and Adam, would you like to add anything to the tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the only thing is you asked Jonathan how you thought it affected him. I think that it's much easier for Adam and myself, that we are survivors because we can understand. You know, we don't have to go into explanations. And I think it's been very supportive for both of us. I think it's been a burden for our children because we have dwelled upon it a lot and because the community, you know, very much saw us as, sort of, the survivor family. And I think it must have been hard for our kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you guys-- my family, it's-- it's-- it never can-- we can never really go or do anything basically without it. I mean, we could be, you know, anywhere. We could be-- it could be any kind of a celebration, but it's never, can never be, you know-- It's like it-- it just always seems to come up. It's like there's always a reason that, you know, my-- my parents would need to-- or my father especially, you know, he just-- he's-- it's made it impossible for him to have a good time when things are, you know, now going so well for him, you know, which kind of sucks. But I guess-- I don't-- I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I want to thank you all for taking part in the taping today. It has been an honor and a pleasure to meet you. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for your hard work because you have to endure it.
